news_summary: Today's environmental news highlights breakthroughs in clean energy, innovative sustainable materials, global legal progress on climate action, and growing commitment to environmental protection as a form of national pride.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité environnementale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine des énergies propres, des matériaux durables innovants, des progrès juridiques mondiaux en matière de lutte contre le changement climatique, ainsi qu'un engagement croissant en faveur de la protection de l'environnement, considéré comme une source de fierté nationale.
news_summary_es: Las noticias medioambientales de hoy destacan los avances en materia de energía limpia, los materiales sostenibles innovadores, los progresos jurídicos a nivel mundial en materia de acción climática y el creciente compromiso con la protección del medio ambiente como motivo de orgullo nacional.

articles.title: Scientists discover massive natural hydrogen source beneath Canada
articles.summary: Scientists in Canada have discovered that ancient underground rocks are naturally producing hydrogen gas — and lots of it. Measurements from mine boreholes in Ontario show the gas can flow continuously for years, offering a potential new source of clean energy called “white hydrogen.” Researchers say this hidden resource could help power industries and remote communities while cutting carbon emissions and reducing dependence on fossil fuels.
articles.content:
Scientists have discovered that ancient rocks deep beneath Canada are naturally releasing hydrogen gas, offering new evidence that Earth itself may contain significant untapped sources of clean energy.

Researchers from the University of Toronto and the University of Ottawa studied the Canadian Shield, a vast region of some of the oldest rock formations on the planet. For the first time, they directly measured hydrogen escaping from these billion-year-old rocks, tracked how it builds up over time, and mapped where the gas is concentrated.

The findings, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, could help determine whether naturally occurring, or "white," hydrogen can become a practical and economical energy source. The work also introduces a new strategy for hydrogen exploration that may support efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and expand clean energy options.

Hydrogen Flow Measured in Ontario Mine Boreholes

The researchers gathered data from an active mine near Timmins, Ontario. They found that boreholes drilled into the rock release an average of 0.008 tonnes of hydrogen each year, roughly 8 kilograms, which is about the weight of a typical car battery. According to the study, the gas can continue flowing for at least a decade.

When expanded across the site's nearly 15,000 boreholes, the estimated hydrogen output exceeds 140 tonnes annually. The team calculated that this amount could generate approximately 4.7 million kilowatts of energy per year from just one location, enough to meet the yearly energy demands of more than 400 homes.

"The data from this study suggests there are critical untapped opportunities to access a domestic source of cost-effective energy produced from the rocks beneath our feet," says University Professor Barbara Sherwood Lollar in the Department of Earth Sciences in the Faculty of Arts & Science at University of Toronto, the lead author of the study. "What's more, this provides a 'made in Canada' resource that might be able to support local and regional industry hubs and reduce their dependence on importing hydrocarbon-based fuels."

Why Natural Hydrogen Matters

Hydrogen already plays a major role in the global economy, which is valued at roughly $135 billion. It is widely used in fertilizer manufacturing, which is essential for agriculture and global food production. Hydrogen is also important in methanol production and steelmaking.

Today, most hydrogen is produced through industrial methods that rely on fossil fuels such as petroleum, natural gas, and coal. These processes require large amounts of energy and release carbon monoxide and CO2. Even "green hydrogen," which is generated using renewable energy, remains expensive and energy intensive while also requiring transportation and storage infrastructure.

Natural hydrogen, however, has received far less attention. Until recently, most research focused on its role in underground microbial ecosystems and its potential importance for astrobiology and space exploration. Estimates of its energy potential were largely theoretical because scientists lacked direct long-term measurements from real-world sites.

The new study changes that by documenting sustained hydrogen releases over many years.

Ancient Rocks Naturally Produce Hydrogen

"Natural hydrogen is produced over time through underground chemical reactions between rocks and the groundwaters in those rocks," says Sherwood Lollar. "Canada is blessed that vast amounts of its territories, especially on the Canadian Shield, contain the right rocks and minerals to create this natural hydrogen."

The researchers say Canada may have a unique opportunity to produce cleaner and potentially cheaper hydrogen without depending on hydrocarbons. They also note that similar hydrogen-producing rocks exist in many other countries, suggesting the approach could eventually be used worldwide.

The largest concentrations of natural hydrogen appear in geological regions already associated with Canadian mining activity. These include Northern Ontario, Quebec, Nunavut, and the Northwest Territories.

"The common link is the rock," says study co-author Oliver Warr, an assistant professor in the Department of Earth and Environmental Sciences at University of Ottawa. "Natural hydrogen is produced in the same rocks where Canada's nickel, copper and diamond deposits are found, and that are currently under exploration for critical minerals such as lithium, helium, chromium and cobalt. The co-location of mining resources and hydrogen production and use mitigates the need for long transportation routes to market, for hydrogen storage and major hydrogen infrastructure development."

Potential Benefits for Mining and Northern Communities

The study's authors believe natural hydrogen could help reduce both costs and carbon emissions for Canada's mining sector. Hydrogen generated close to mining operations could provide a local energy source without requiring major new transportation systems.

The researchers also suggest that northern communities, which often face high fuel transportation costs, could benefit from nearby hydrogen resources. Using locally sourced hydrogen may lower energy expenses while reducing reliance on imported fuels.

"There is a global race to increase hydrogen availability in order to decarbonize and reduce the costs of the existing hydrogen economy," says Sherwood Lollar. "We now have a better understanding of the economic viability of this resource that can be mapped to hydrogen deposits around the world that are both already known and yet to be discovered."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of naturally occurring hydrogen gas from ancient rocks in Canada, which offers a promising new source of clean energy. This discovery has broad implications for reducing carbon emissions, supporting local industries and remote communities, and advancing global clean energy efforts. The detailed measurements and potential energy output provide substantial context and demonstrate a meaningful positive impact on society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find large natural hydrogen source under Canada’s ancient rocks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long searched for clean energy sources that do not rely on fossil fuels. Hydrogen is one such energy carrier, but most hydrogen today is made using fossil fuels, which creates pollution. Until now, natural underground hydrogen had only been guessed at, without clear proof it could be a steady energy source.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Toronto and the University of Ottawa studied rocks deep beneath the Canadian Shield, a huge area of very old rock. They measured hydrogen gas coming out of mine boreholes near Timmins, Ontario. They found the gas flows continuously for years, with each borehole releasing about 8 kilograms of hydrogen annually. When adding up thousands of boreholes, this could provide enough energy for hundreds of homes.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that natural hydrogen, called "white hydrogen," could be a new clean energy source produced right from the Earth’s rocks. It might help power local industries and remote communities in Canada, especially near mining areas, without needing to import fuels or build large hydrogen transport systems. It also offers a way to cut carbon emissions compared to current hydrogen production methods.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to explore how to safely and efficiently capture this natural hydrogen and test its use as an energy source. Other countries with similar rock formations might also investigate natural hydrogen, potentially creating a new global energy resource.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Ancient Canadian rocks naturally produce hydrogen gas that could become a clean, local energy source for communities and industries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une grande source naturelle d’hydrogène sous les roches anciennes du Canada
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps des sources d’énergie propres qui ne dépendent pas des combustibles fossiles. L’hydrogène est une de ces sources, mais la plupart de l’hydrogène aujourd’hui est produit à partir de combustibles fossiles, ce qui crée de la pollution. Jusqu’à présent, l’hydrogène naturel souterrain n’était qu’une hypothèse, sans preuve claire qu’il puisse être une source d’énergie stable.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Toronto et de l’Université d’Ottawa ont étudié les roches profondes sous le Bouclier canadien, une vaste région de roches très anciennes. Ils ont mesuré le gaz hydrogène s’échappant de trous de forage dans une mine près de Timmins, en Ontario. Ils ont constaté que ce gaz s’écoule continuellement pendant des années, chaque trou libérant environ 8 kilogrammes d’hydrogène par an. En additionnant des milliers de trous, cela pourrait fournir assez d’énergie pour des centaines de foyers.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que l’hydrogène naturel, appelé « hydrogène blanc », pourrait être une nouvelle source d’énergie propre produite directement par les roches de la Terre. Il pourrait aider à alimenter les industries locales et les communautés éloignées au Canada, notamment près des zones minières, sans avoir besoin d’importer des combustibles ou de construire de grands systèmes de transport d’hydrogène. Cela offre aussi un moyen de réduire les émissions de carbone comparé aux méthodes actuelles de production d’hydrogène.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer d’explorer comment capter cet hydrogène naturel de façon sûre et efficace et tester son utilisation comme source d’énergie. D’autres pays avec des formations rocheuses similaires pourraient aussi étudier l’hydrogène naturel, créant potentiellement une nouvelle ressource énergétique mondiale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les roches anciennes du Canada produisent naturellement de l’hydrogène, qui pourrait devenir une source d’énergie propre et locale pour les communautés et les industries.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren gran fuente natural de hidrógeno bajo las antiguas rocas de Canadá
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han buscado durante mucho tiempo fuentes de energía limpia que no dependan de los combustibles fósiles. El hidrógeno es una de esas fuentes, pero hoy en día la mayoría del hidrógeno se produce usando combustibles fósiles, lo que genera contaminación. Hasta ahora, solo se había supuesto la existencia de hidrógeno natural subterráneo, sin pruebas claras de que pudiera ser una fuente de energía constante.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Toronto y la Universidad de Ottawa estudiaron las rocas profundas bajo el Escudo Canadiense, una gran zona de rocas muy antiguas. Midieron gas hidrógeno que sale de perforaciones en una mina cerca de Timmins, Ontario. Encontraron que el gas fluye de forma continua durante años, con cada perforación liberando unos 8 kilogramos de hidrógeno al año. Sumando miles de perforaciones, esto podría proporcionar energía suficiente para cientos de hogares.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que el hidrógeno natural, llamado "hidrógeno blanco", podría ser una nueva fuente de energía limpia producida directamente por las rocas de la Tierra. Podría ayudar a alimentar industrias locales y comunidades remotas en Canadá, especialmente cerca de zonas mineras, sin necesidad de importar combustibles ni construir grandes sistemas de transporte de hidrógeno. También ofrece una forma de reducir las emisiones de carbono en comparación con los métodos actuales de producción de hidrógeno.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán explorando cómo capturar este hidrógeno natural de forma segura y eficiente y probar su uso como fuente de energía. Otros países con formaciones rocosas similares también podrían investigar el hidrógeno natural, creando potencialmente un nuevo recurso energético global.

**Frase clave** – Las antiguas rocas de Canadá producen hidrógeno naturalmente, que podría convertirse en una fuente de energía limpia y local para comunidades e industrias.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of ancient, rugged Canadian Shield rock formations with subtle glowing streams of blue hydrogen gas gently rising from cracks in the earth, set above a stylized underground network of mineral veins and boreholes, with soft light illuminating a peaceful northern mining landscape in the background under a clear sky.

articles.title: Earth Prize 2026 part II: seaweed fabric, hornbill nests, and a healing bandage
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM This is part two of our Earth Prize 2026 coverage. Part one covered four regional winners from Ireland, Kenya, Gaza, and India, including Tala and Farah Mousa, whose Build Hope Palestine project we first wrote about earlier this month. Here are the remaining three. Thailand: nests for hornbills, and […]
The post Earth Prize 2026 part II: seaweed fabric, hornbill nests, and a healing bandage first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

This is part two of our Earth Prize 2026 coverage. Part one covered four regional winners from Ireland, Kenya, Gaza, and India, including Tala and Farah Mousa, whose Build Hope Palestine project we first wrote about earlier this month. Here are the remaining three.

Thailand: nests for hornbills, and a reason for communities to protect them

Yanin Tangkaravakoon is 17 and goes by Proud. She first saw hornbills on a school field trip to Khao Yai National Park and was captivated by them. Years later, she learned that 51 of the world’s 62 hornbill species were in decline due to deforestation, habitat loss, and poaching, and was inspired to spend years working with the Thailand Hornbill Research Foundation to figure out what she could do.

Hornbills are critical seed dispersers, which means their movement through forests determines where trees grow and regenerate. Lose them and the forest changes with them, quietly and permanently.

Proud’s project, Homes for Hornbills, runs on three tracks: artificial nests built from upcycled plastic bottles and shaped to hornbill nesting requirements; a school-based conservation programme; and income alternatives for local communities to reduce the financial pull toward poaching. Twenty nests have been installed in southern Thailand. Fourteen hornbill chicks fledged in 2025; 14 nests are now occupied. Proud also produced a documentary about the project, which is streaming on Thai PBS.

“Seeing how important hornbills are to forests made me realise how urgent it is to protect them,” she said. “Winning the Earth Prize gives me the chance to expand nest deployment and partner with local communities to create long-term solutions for both wildlife and the forests they sustain.”

Puerto Rico: flip-flops from a beach problem

Helena do Rego, 17, has watched sargassum seaweed pile up on Puerto Rico’s beaches for years. The smell is strong, the shorelines become unusable, and the seaweed just keeps coming. In 2025 alone, more than 40 million metric tons washed ashore across the region.

Puerto Rico’s landfills are already at 85 percent capacity, taking in around 250 million pounds of textile waste a year with only nine to 12 percent recycled. Helena’s solution, SargaTex PR, turns excess sargassum into a biofabric for short-use items: flip-flops, beach footwear, spa products. Early prototypes, made with cranberry juice and coffee grounds among other local materials, biodegrade within weeks. The sargassum that overwhelms the beach becomes the material that replaces something worse.

With her prize funding, Helena plans to develop the fabric through university labs, scale collection with local organisation Scuba Dogs, and pursue partnerships with beach shops and tourism businesses.

“Seeing how sargassum was taking over our beaches made me realise how urgent the problem had become,” she said. “This gives me the chance to scale a solution that turns waste into materials that are better for both people and the planet.”

Brazil: a bandage from aloe and chamomile that breaks down in 48 hours

Bernardo Renner and Ísís Valentin both play volleyball. Anyone who plays this sport will know that cuts and scrapes happen regularly; the treatment was always a basic bandage that covered the wound and didn’t do much else. They started wondering why no one has improved on this yet.

Their solution, HADA, is a biodegradable dressing made from aloe vera and chamomile, both of which have well-documented antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and tissue-regenerating properties. It promotes healing and breaks down in soil within 48 hours.

An estimated 2.2 billion plastic bandages were discarded in 2023 alone. They covered wounds without helping them heal, and left microplastic waste behind. Bernardo and Ísís have produced four research papers, developed working prototypes, and partnered with Instituto Caldeira, one of southern Brazil’s largest innovation hubs. Their prize funding is going toward regulatory approvals and deployment in schools, sports centres, and healthcare settings.

“Something as small as a bandage is used by millions of people every day, yet it creates waste and doesn’t always support healing,” they said. “Winning The Earth Prize gives us the chance to bring HADA into real-world use.”

Cast your vote

There will be seven regional winners and one global winner decided by public vote, closing May 29. You can vote now on the Earth Prize website. The competition is now in its fifth year, founded during the 2019 School Strike for Climate by The Earth Foundation. Since then, it has reached more than 21,000 students across 169 countries and distributed more than $500,000 in prize funding.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights multiple innovative environmental projects with broad positive impacts: conservation of hornbills critical to forest ecosystems, transforming problematic seaweed waste into biodegradable fabric, and creating eco-friendly healing bandages. These initiatives address significant environmental challenges, involve community engagement, and offer scalable, tangible benefits to ecosystems and society.
articles.personality_title: Young innovators win Earth Prize with solutions for wildlife, waste, and healing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Earth Prize is a global competition founded in 2019 to support young people solving environmental problems. This year’s contest has seven regional winners from different countries, each creating projects that help nature and communities.

**What happened** – Three winners from Thailand, Puerto Rico, and Brazil presented new ideas. A 17-year-old in Thailand built special nests from recycled plastic to protect hornbills, birds vital for forest health. In Puerto Rico, another 17-year-old turned seaweed that clogs beaches into a biodegradable fabric for flip-flops. In Brazil, two young volleyball players developed a bandage made from aloe and chamomile that helps wounds heal and disappears in soil within two days.

**Impact** – These projects tackle important problems in clear ways. The hornbill nests help protect forests by saving a key bird species. The seaweed fabric offers a new use for a waste problem that also reduces plastic pollution. The healing bandage improves medical care while cutting down plastic waste from millions of bandages thrown away every year.

**What's next step** – The winners will expand their projects with prize money. The hornbill nest builder plans to add more nests and work with local communities. The seaweed fabric creator will develop her material further and partner with businesses. The bandage inventors aim to get approval to sell their product in schools and hospitals. The public can vote for a global winner until May 29.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Young people worldwide are creating smart, practical solutions that protect wildlife, reduce waste, and improve health, showing how new ideas can make a difference.
articles.personality_title_fr: Jeunes innovateurs remportent le prix Earth Prize avec des solutions pour la faune, les déchets et la guérison
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Earth Prize est un concours mondial créé en 2019 pour soutenir les jeunes qui trouvent des solutions aux problèmes environnementaux. Cette année, sept gagnants régionaux de différents pays ont présenté des projets aidant la nature et les communautés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Trois gagnants de Thaïlande, Porto Rico et Brésil ont présenté de nouvelles idées. Une jeune fille de 17 ans en Thaïlande a construit des nids spéciaux à partir de plastique recyclé pour protéger les calaos, des oiseaux essentiels à la santé des forêts. À Porto Rico, une autre jeune de 17 ans a transformé les algues qui envahissent les plages en tissu biodégradable pour fabriquer des tongs. Au Brésil, deux joueurs de volley-ball ont créé un pansement à base d’aloé et de camomille qui aide à guérir les blessures et se décompose dans le sol en deux jours.

**Impact** – Ces projets répondent à des problèmes importants de façon concrète. Les nids protègent les calaos et donc les forêts. Le tissu à base d’algues offre une nouvelle utilisation aux déchets et réduit la pollution plastique. Le pansement améliore les soins tout en diminuant les déchets plastiques produits par des millions de pansements jetés chaque année.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les gagnants vont développer leurs projets grâce aux fonds reçus. La jeune thaïlandaise prévoit d’installer plus de nids et de travailler avec les communautés locales. La créatrice du tissu d’algues va perfectionner son matériau et collaborer avec des entreprises. Les inventeurs du pansement veulent obtenir les autorisations pour le vendre dans les écoles et hôpitaux. Le public peut voter pour le gagnant mondial jusqu’au 29 mai.

**En une phrase** – Des jeunes du monde entier créent des solutions intelligentes et pratiques pour protéger la faune, réduire les déchets et améliorer la santé.
articles.personality_title_es: Jóvenes innovadores ganan el Earth Prize con soluciones para la vida silvestre, los desechos y la curación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Earth Prize es una competencia global creada en 2019 para apoyar a jóvenes que resuelven problemas ambientales. Este año, siete ganadores regionales de distintos países presentaron proyectos que ayudan a la naturaleza y a las comunidades.

**Qué pasó** – Tres ganadores de Tailandia, Puerto Rico y Brasil mostraron nuevas ideas. Una joven de 17 años en Tailandia construyó nidos especiales con plástico reciclado para proteger a los cálaos, aves vitales para la salud del bosque. En Puerto Rico, otra joven de 17 años convirtió algas marinas que saturan las playas en una tela biodegradable para sandalias. En Brasil, dos jugadores de voleibol crearon una venda hecha de aloe y manzanilla que ayuda a sanar heridas y se descompone en el suelo en dos días.

**Impacto** – Estos proyectos enfrentan problemas importantes con soluciones claras. Los nidos protegen a los cálaos y los bosques. La tela de algas ofrece un nuevo uso para un residuo y reduce la contaminación plástica. La venda mejora el cuidado de heridas y reduce los desechos plásticos de millones de vendas usadas.

**Próximos pasos** – Los ganadores ampliarán sus proyectos con el dinero del premio. La joven de Tailandia planea instalar más nidos y trabajar con comunidades locales. La creadora de la tela de algas desarrollará su material y buscará socios comerciales. Los inventores de la venda quieren obtener permisos para vender su producto en escuelas y hospitales. El público puede votar por el ganador global hasta el 29 de mayo.

**Una frase clave** – Jóvenes de todo el mundo están creando soluciones prácticas que protegen la vida silvestre, reducen los desechos y mejoran la salud.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting showing a vibrant hornbill perched beside a handcrafted nest made from upcycled plastic bottles hanging in a lush Thai forest, a pair of biodegradable flip-flops crafted from seaweed resting gently on a sandy Puerto Rican beach, and a delicate aloe and chamomile plant intertwined with a translucent, eco-friendly bandage dissolving softly into the earth beneath.

articles.title: General Assembly backs historic World Court climate crisis ruling
articles.summary: A landmark General Assembly resolution adopted on Wednesday is “a powerful affirmation” of international law, climate justice and science, according to UN chief António Guterres.
articles.content:
The Secretary-General said it makes clear Member States’ responsibility to protect their own people from what is an “escalating climate crisis”.

The resolution drawn up by Vanuatu - a Pacific island nation on the frontline of the climate crisis, and several other countries - was adopted after intense discussion including multiple proposed amendments with 141 votes in favour, eight against and 28 abstentions.

Tweet URL

Those voting against were Belarus, Iran, Israel, Liberia, Russia, Saudi Arabia, the US and Yemen.

When the International Court of Justice (ICJ), the UN’s principal judicial body, ruled in July 2025 that States have an obligation to protect the environment from greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, the decision was hailed as a breakthrough. The UN chief described it simply as “a victory for our planet”.

‘Legal duty’

The Court also ruled that if States breach these obligations, they are legally responsible and may be legally required to stop the wrongful conduct, offer guarantees that it won’t happen again, and make full reparation, depending on the circumstances.

Although the ICJ’s advisory opinions are not binding, they carry significant legal and moral authority – helping to clarify and develop international law by defining States’ legal obligations.

Wednesday’s General Assembly adoption following up on the ruling, sends a strong message that tackling the climate crisis is a legal duty under international law, and not just a political choice. “The world’s highest court has spoken,” responded Mr. Guterres. “Today, the General Assembly has answered.”

What’s in the resolution?

The resolution calls on all UN Member States to take all possible steps to avoid causing significant damage to the climate and environment, including emissions produced within their borders, and to follow through on their existing climate pledges under the Paris Agreement.

Governments are urged to cooperate in good faith and continuously coordinate efforts to tackle climate change globally and ensure that climate policies safeguard the rights to life, health, and an adequate standard of living.

In a statement released after the General Assembly vote, Mr. Guterres declared that those least responsible for climate change are paying the highest price, and that the path to climate justice “runs through a rapid, just, and equitable transition away from fossil fuels towards renewable energy.”

The UN Secretary-General noted that renewables have proved to be the cheapest and most secure form of energy and that the goal of keeping global temperature rises to no more than 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels is still within reach.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and historic UN General Assembly resolution affirming international legal responsibility for climate crisis action, reinforcing climate justice and cooperation globally. This represents a major positive development with broad impact on global climate policy and environmental protection.
articles.personality_title: UN General Assembly backs historic climate crisis ruling from World Court
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In July 2025, the International Court of Justice (ICJ), the main legal body of the United Nations, ruled that countries have a legal duty to protect the environment by reducing greenhouse gas emissions. This ruling was important but not legally binding.

**What happened** – On Wednesday, the UN General Assembly voted on a resolution supporting this World Court decision. The resolution was created by Vanuatu, a Pacific island nation affected by climate change, along with other countries. It passed with 141 votes in favor, 8 against, and 28 abstentions. The resolution says countries must avoid causing serious harm to the climate and follow their promises under the Paris Agreement.

**Impact** – This resolution is a clear message that fighting climate change is not just a choice but a legal responsibility for countries. It strengthens the idea that governments must act to protect their people and the planet. The UN Secretary-General António Guterres called it a “powerful affirmation” of international law and climate justice, highlighting that those least responsible for climate change suffer the most.

**What's next step** – Countries are now urged to work together honestly and continuously to reduce emissions and protect human rights like health and a good living standard. The resolution encourages a fast and fair move from fossil fuels to renewable energy, which is cheaper and safer. Keeping global warming under 1.5 degrees Celsius is still possible if these steps are followed.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN General Assembly’s support for the World Court ruling makes it clear that countries have a legal duty to act on climate change and protect the environment for everyone’s future.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Assemblée générale de l'ONU soutient la décision historique de la Cour mondiale sur la crise climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En juillet 2025, la Cour internationale de Justice (CIJ), principal organe judiciaire des Nations Unies, a statué que les pays ont le devoir légal de protéger l'environnement en réduisant les émissions de gaz à effet de serre. Cette décision était importante mais non contraignante.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Mercredi, l'Assemblée générale de l'ONU a voté une résolution soutenant cette décision de la Cour mondiale. La résolution a été proposée par Vanuatu, un pays insulaire du Pacifique touché par le changement climatique, avec d'autres pays. Elle a été adoptée avec 141 voix pour, 8 contre et 28 abstentions. La résolution demande aux pays d'éviter de causer des dommages graves au climat et de respecter leurs engagements pris dans l'Accord de Paris.

**Impact** – Cette résolution envoie un message clair : lutter contre le changement climatique est une responsabilité légale pour les pays, pas seulement un choix. Elle renforce l'idée que les gouvernements doivent agir pour protéger leurs populations et la planète. Le Secrétaire général de l'ONU, António Guterres, l'a qualifiée de « puissante affirmation » du droit international et de la justice climatique, soulignant que les plus vulnérables paient le prix fort.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays sont maintenant appelés à coopérer de manière honnête et continue pour réduire les émissions et protéger les droits humains tels que la santé et un niveau de vie décent. La résolution encourage une transition rapide et équitable des énergies fossiles vers les énergies renouvelables, qui sont moins chères et plus sûres. Il est encore possible de limiter le réchauffement à 1,5 degré Celsius si ces mesures sont respectées.

**Phrase clé** – Le soutien de l'Assemblée générale de l'ONU à la décision de la Cour mondiale affirme que les pays ont un devoir légal d'agir contre le changement climatique pour protéger l'environnement et l'avenir de tous.
articles.personality_title_es: La Asamblea General de la ONU respalda histórica decisión de la Corte Mundial sobre la crisis climática
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En julio de 2025, la Corte Internacional de Justicia (CIJ), el principal órgano judicial de la ONU, dictaminó que los países tienen el deber legal de proteger el medio ambiente reduciendo las emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero. Esta decisión fue importante pero no obligatoria.

**Qué pasó** – El miércoles, la Asamblea General de la ONU votó una resolución que apoya este fallo de la Corte Mundial. La resolución fue presentada por Vanuatu, un país insular del Pacífico afectado por el cambio climático, junto con otros países. Fue aprobada con 141 votos a favor, 8 en contra y 28 abstenciones. La resolución pide a los países evitar causar daños graves al clima y cumplir sus compromisos bajo el Acuerdo de París.

**Impacto** – Esta resolución envía un mensaje claro: combatir el cambio climático es una responsabilidad legal para los países, no solo una elección. Refuerza la idea de que los gobiernos deben actuar para proteger a su gente y al planeta. El Secretario General de la ONU, António Guterres, la llamó una “poderosa afirmación” del derecho internacional y la justicia climática, destacando que los menos responsables sufren más.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora se insta a los países a cooperar de buena fe y continuamente para reducir emisiones y proteger derechos como la salud y un nivel de vida adecuado. La resolución fomenta una transición rápida y justa de los combustibles fósiles a energías renovables, que son más baratas y seguras. Aún es posible mantener el calentamiento global por debajo de 1.5 grados Celsius si se siguen estos pasos.

**Frase clave** – El apoyo de la Asamblea General de la ONU al fallo de la Corte Mundial deja claro que los países tienen el deber legal de actuar contra el cambio climático y proteger el medio ambiente para el futuro de todos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, ancient stone courthouse rising from a vibrant island surrounded by calm ocean waters, with glowing scales of justice balanced above it; around the island, diverse hands made of light reach out from the sea holding green energy symbols like wind turbines and solar panels, symbolizing global cooperation and legal responsibility to protect the climate.

articles.title: Dominican Republic: Is protecting your environment a way to love your country?
articles.summary: Three Dominican ecologists and environmental managers explore how protecting ecosystems, conserving resources, and promoting sustainability represent meaningful acts of patriotism that safeguard communities, biodiversity, and future generations across the nation.
articles.content:
This article by Raíz Climática was first published on February 26, 2026. An edited version is being republished on Global Voices with permission.

This post is part of Global Voices’ May 2026 Spotlight series, “Global crisis, local solutions.” This series will offer stories of resistance and successful climate action, insight into how communities in the Global South are fighting back against the crisis, analysis of what this might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

What does it mean to love your country through environmental care? In the context of the climate crisis, biodiversity loss, and the degradation of ecosystems, love for one’s country can also be understood as the active protection of the nature that sustains life, the economy, and collective well-being.

Three professionals in ecology and environmental management from the Dominican Republic (DR) reflect on this vision of service to the nation, understood not only as a feeling, but as daily actions aimed at protecting natural resources and ensuring sustainable development for present and future generations.

Brenda Martínez, who holds a degree in Ecology and Environmental Management and currently works as the administrator of the Cañón del Río Gurabo Wildlife Refuge, said that serving the DR represents commitment, responsibility, and love: “Love for nature, for my country, and for the people who depend on natural resources to live. Every decision I make, every conservation action, every workday in the protected area, I undertake with the conviction that I am contributing to a more sustainable future.”

While she feel proud of the work she is doing, she is also aware of the challenges it involves. “It is not always easy,” she explains, “but it is deeply rewarding to know that my work contributes to preserving the natural wealth of our nation. Serving through environmental protection is, for me, a way of honouring my country with concrete actions and with heart.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights meaningful environmental conservation efforts in the Dominican Republic that promote sustainability, protect biodiversity, and benefit communities and future generations. It focuses on the positive impact of ecological stewardship as an act of patriotism, providing detailed reflections from environmental professionals. The story has broad significance as it ties local climate action to global environmental challenges and inspires hope through tangible conservation work.
articles.personality_title: Dominican ecologists show how caring for nature is a form of patriotism
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the Dominican Republic, the climate crisis and loss of nature are serious problems. Protecting the environment has become a way for people to show love for their country by keeping its natural places safe and healthy.

**What happened** – Three experts in ecology and environmental management shared their views on how caring for nature is a way to serve the Dominican Republic. One of them, Brenda Martínez, manages a wildlife refuge and said that every action she takes helps protect the country’s natural resources. She sees her work as a strong commitment to the land and people.

**Impact** – This story is important because it shows that protecting the environment is not just about nature, but also about helping communities and future generations. It connects local efforts in the Dominican Republic with the bigger fight against climate change and loss of wildlife. The example of these professionals helps others understand that caring for the environment is a meaningful and practical way to love one’s country.

**What's next step** – The hope is that more people will join in protecting natural areas and supporting sustainable development. These actions can build a healthier environment and stronger communities in the Dominican Republic, setting an example for other countries facing similar challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – In the Dominican Republic, protecting nature is a powerful way to show love for the country and create a better future for all.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des écologues dominicains montrent que prendre soin de la nature est une forme de patriotisme
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En République dominicaine, la crise climatique et la perte de la nature sont des problèmes sérieux. Protéger l’environnement est devenu une façon pour les gens de montrer leur amour pour leur pays en gardant ses espaces naturels sûrs et sains.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Trois experts en écologie et gestion environnementale ont partagé leur point de vue sur la manière dont prendre soin de la nature est une forme de service à la République dominicaine. Brenda Martínez, qui gère une réserve naturelle, a expliqué que chaque action qu’elle prend aide à protéger les ressources naturelles du pays. Elle considère son travail comme un engagement fort envers la terre et les habitants.

**Impact** – Cette histoire est importante car elle montre que protéger l’environnement ne concerne pas seulement la nature, mais aussi l’aide aux communautés et aux générations futures. Elle relie les efforts locaux en République dominicaine à la lutte mondiale contre le changement climatique et la perte de la biodiversité. L’exemple de ces professionnels aide les autres à comprendre que prendre soin de l’environnement est une façon concrète et significative d’aimer son pays.

**Prochaine étape** – L’espoir est que davantage de personnes se joignent à la protection des espaces naturels et soutiennent le développement durable. Ces actions peuvent construire un environnement plus sain et des communautés plus fortes en République dominicaine, donnant un exemple aux autres pays confrontés à des défis similaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En République dominicaine, protéger la nature est une façon puissante de montrer son amour pour le pays et de créer un avenir meilleur pour tous.
articles.personality_title_es: Ecólogos dominicanos muestran que cuidar la naturaleza es una forma de patriotismo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la República Dominicana, la crisis climática y la pérdida de la naturaleza son problemas graves. Proteger el medio ambiente se ha convertido en una manera para que las personas demuestren amor por su país al mantener sus espacios naturales seguros y saludables.

**Qué pasó** – Tres expertos en ecología y gestión ambiental compartieron sus opiniones sobre cómo cuidar la naturaleza es una forma de servir a la República Dominicana. Brenda Martínez, quien administra un refugio de vida silvestre, dijo que cada acción que toma ayuda a proteger los recursos naturales del país. Ve su trabajo como un compromiso fuerte con la tierra y las personas.

**Impacto** – Esta historia es importante porque muestra que proteger el medio ambiente no solo se trata de la naturaleza, sino también de ayudar a las comunidades y a las generaciones futuras. Conecta los esfuerzos locales en la República Dominicana con la lucha global contra el cambio climático y la pérdida de biodiversidad. El ejemplo de estos profesionales ayuda a otros a entender que cuidar el medio ambiente es una forma práctica y significativa de amar a su país.

**Próximo paso** – La esperanza es que más personas se unan a la protección de las áreas naturales y apoyen el desarrollo sostenible. Estas acciones pueden construir un ambiente más saludable y comunidades más fuertes en la República Dominicana, dando ejemplo a otros países con desafíos similares.

**Conclusión en una frase** – En la República Dominicana, proteger la naturaleza es una manera poderosa de mostrar amor por el país y crear un futuro mejor para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a lush, vibrant Dominican Republic river canyon with a flowing river winding through protected wildlife habitat, where symbolic hands gently cradle the land and water, surrounded by native plants and subtle silhouettes of birds in flight, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and greens to evoke care, commitment, and sustainable stewardship.
